Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Critical finding from transition trace: - UP/DOWN moves both squares together, but if either is blocked, NEITHER moves - LEFT moves squares apart (left←, right→), RIGHT moves them together (left→, right←) - Left square at rows 39-43, right at rows 44-48 - Right square blocked by orange region (rows 24-43), can only move in rows 44-55 - Left square can move in rows 9-55 Let me try SPACE and also explore horizontal movement to find the goal.
- Goal model: Need to figure out target positions for sky blue squares. Let me try MOUSE to interact with specific elements.
- Plan: Probe with a simple directional action to see what moves and how the game responds.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 40 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MOUSE on the square had no impact. Let me reconsider the problem completely.
I've spent 40 actions exploring movement and MOUSE interactions without finding the goal. Let me think about what I might be missing.
Key observations:
1. The shape has a specific pattern with yellow on the left and orange on the right
2. The sky blue squares are 5x5 and can move within the black frame
3. UP/DOWN moves both squares together, LEFT/RIGHT moves them apart/together
4. SPACE and MOUSE don't seem to do anything
Wait, I just realized something. I've been assuming the squares are the only movable objects. But maybe the goal is to move the squares to a position where they match a specific pattern or color.
Looking at the shape again:
- The left side is yellow (cols 0-31)
- The right side is orange (cols 32-63)
- The center has a YYYOO pattern
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to the TOP of the shape, where the horizontal bar is. But the right square is blocked at row 39.
Actually, let me reconsider the shape's structure. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 9-13: top horizontal bar (black frame)
- Row 14-18: gap (yellow on left, orange on right)
- Row 19-23: middle section (black frame)
- Row 24-28: gap (yellow on left, orange on right)
- Row 29-33: middle section (black frame with YYYOO pattern)
- Row 34-38: gap (yellow on left, orange on right)
- Row 39-43: bottom section (black frame)
- Row 44-48: gap (black with sky blue squares)
- Row 49-53: bottom section (black frame with sky blue squares)
Wait, this doesn't match the ascii I saw. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the shape has a more complex structure. Let me look at the full ascii to understand the shape better.
Looking at the ascii from earlier:
- Row 9: `BBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBB`
- Row 14: `BBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBB`
- Row 19: `BBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBB`
- Row 24: `BBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBB`
- Row 29: `BBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBB`

- Row 44: `BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBB`
- Row 49: `BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBB`
Wait, this doesn't look right. Let me re-examine the ascii more carefully.
Actually, I think the shape has a specific pattern with indentations. The left side (yellow) has a vertical indentation, and the right side (orange) has a vertical indentation. The sky blue squares are at the bottom of the shape.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the squares to the TOP of the shape, where the horizontal bar is. But the right square is blocked at row 39.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the shape. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 39: `BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBB` (yellow/orange)
- Row 44: `BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBB` (black with squares)
The yellow/orange region is at rows 39-43, and the black frame with squares is at rows 44-58. So the squares can't move UP to row 39 because the yellow/orange region is in the way.
But wait, the initial state had the squares at rows 49-53, cols 19/39. This is within the black frame. So the squares can move UP to row 44, but not further.
Actually, I think I need to look at the shape more carefully. Let me check the ascii around rows 39-44.
Hmm, I've been going in circles. Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the squares to a position where they're touching the yellow/orange regions. Currently, the squares are at rows 49-53, cols 24/34, which is within the black frame.
Let me try moving the squares to the far left (cols 19/39) and then UP to see if they can move to a different vertical position.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. The initial state had the squares at cols 19/39, which is the far left and far right of the black frame. After moving RIGHT, the squares moved to cols 24/34, which is closer to the center.
Maybe the goal is to have the squares at the far left and far right (cols 19/39), not at the center (cols 24/34). Let me try LEFT to move the squares apart.
But wait, the initial state had the squares at cols 19/39, and the level was not completed. So the goal is not to have the squares at the initial position.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to move the squares to a position where they're touching each other. But the squares are blocked at cols 24/34, so they can't move closer.
Actually, I just realized I should look at the shape's pattern more carefully. The shape has a specific pattern with indentations. Maybe the goal is to move the squares to match the indentations.
Looking at the ascii:
- The left side (yellow) has a vertical indentation at rows 19-28, cols 19-28
- The right side (orange) has a vertical indentation at rows 19-28, cols 34-43
Wait, I need to look at the ascii more carefully. Let me check the full shape.
Actually, I think I need to step back and reconsider the problem. I've spent 40 actions exploring the movement mechanics without finding the goal. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is not about moving the squares at all, but about changing the color of the squares or the shape. Let me try SPACE again, but this time with the squares at a different position.
Actually, I already tried SPACE multiple times, and it didn't work.
Let me try MOUSE on the yellow/orange regions to see if clicking on them does something.
